subject: English
q: What was the last item that Jupie needed to collect?
skill: Alien Invasion - Recognize science fiction as a text type; identify the purpose and audience of the text
feedback: The correct answer is one dandelion. Jupie needed only one dandelion.

subject: English
q: Why did Jupie have to shrink Dino to pick him up?
options: Dino and Jupie were the same size
Jupie didn't want to hurt Dino
skill: Alien Invasion - Recognize science fiction as a text type; identify the purpose and audience of the text
feedback: The correct answer is Dino and Jupie were the same size. When Dino was back to his usual size, he realised that Jupie was no taller than him. Jupie would have had a hard time picking up someone who is the same size as him.

subject: English
q: Complete this sentence. "Jupie frowned because it was..."
options: angry that its spaceship was broken.
worried that Dino's parents would wake up.
worried that it may not be able to go home.
skill: Alien Invasion - Recognize science fiction as a text type; identify the purpose and audience of the text
feedback: The correct answer is worried that it may not be able to go home. Jupie frowned when it told Dino that unless it found the last item, it can't go home.

subject: English
q: Why couldn't Dino scream for help?
options: he had his voice taken away
he was too scared to scream
skill: Alien Invasion - Recognize science fiction as a text type; identify the purpose and audience of the text
feedback: The correct answer is he wasn't loud enough. Dino had been shrunk and was now the size of a paperclip--he was too small and too soft to be heard.

subject: English
q: Dino's body felt like 'jelly' when he got zapped. What is another word to describe this feeling?
skill: Alien Invasion - Recognize science fiction as a text type; identify the purpose and audience of the text
feedback: The correct answer is wobbly. Dino's body felt like jelly and he felt it wobble.

subject: English
q: What did Jupie use to make Dino shrink?
skill: Alien Invasion - Recognize science fiction as a text type; identify the purpose and audience of the text
feedback: The correct answer is a glow stick. Jupie pointed a glow stick at Dino. Dino heard a zap and felt a shock run through his body. Then he started to shrink.

subject: English
q: What woke Dino from his sleep?
options: a bright blue glow and the sound of footsteps
a bright blue glow and a whisper
a bright blue glow and someone clearing their throat
the bright streetlights and the sound of footsteps
skill: Alien Invasion - Recognize science fiction as a text type; identify the purpose and audience of the text
feedback: The correct answer is a bright blue glow and the sound of footsteps . Dino woke up to a bright blue glow from beneath his door and the sound of footsteps.

subject: English
q: If the sentence contains a modal verb, where does the adverb of frequency go?
options: between the modal verb and the main verb
skill: Always and Never - Identify and use adverbs of frequency
feedback: The correct answer is "between the modal verb and the main verb". The sentence should follow this structure: subject, modal verb, adverb of frequency, and main verb.

subject: English
q: Which sentence is correct?
options: I am bored rarely in science class.
I am rarely bored in science class.
I am bored in science class rarely.
I rarely bored am in science class.
skill: Always and Never - Identify and use adverbs of frequency
feedback: The correct answer is "I am rarely bored in science class". The adverb "rarely" comes after the subject and the to be verb "am".

subject: English
q:
Choose the correct adverb of frequency.
I always remember to do my homework. I _____ forget.
skill: Always and Never - Identify and use adverbs of frequency
feedback: The correct answer is "never". The adverb of frequency "never" is the opposite of "always". The speaker always remembers to do his homework; he never forgets.

subject: English
q:
Choose the correct adverb of frequency.
I almost never see the doctor. I _____ fall sick.
skill: Always and Never - Identify and use adverbs of frequency
feedback: The correct answer is "rarely". The adverb of frequency "rarely" tells us that something almost never happens.

subject: English
q:
Choose the correct adverb of frequency.
It's rude not to say thank you. You should _____ say thank you.
skill: Always and Never - Identify and use adverbs of frequency
feedback: The correct answer is "always". "You should always say thank you." The adverb of frequency "always" tells us that it should happen all the time, because it's "rude not to say it".

subject: English
q:
Choose the correct adverb of frequency.
We ______ go to the theatre. I like to watch a movie every once in a while.
skill: Always and Never - Identify and use adverbs of frequency
feedback: The correct answer is "sometimes". The adverb of frequency "sometimes" tells us that something happens every once in a while.

subject: English
q: Which sentence contains an adverb of frequency?
options: I like to snack all day,
I snack a lot all day.
skill: Always and Never - Identify and use adverbs of frequency
feedback: The correct answer is "I often snack all day." The adverb of frequency is "often".

subject: English
q: Which sentence contains an adverb of frequency?
options: I keep forgetting what day it is.
I forgot what day it is today.
I sometimes forget what day it is.
skill: Always and Never - Identify and use adverbs of frequency
feedback: The correct answer is "I sometimes forget what day it is." The adverb of frequency is "sometimes".

subject: English
q:
Read the sentence. Identify the adverb of frequency.
My parents are rarely unhappy about my grades.
skill: Always and Never - Identify and use adverbs of frequency
feedback: The correct answer is "rarely". The adverb of frequency is "rarely".

subject: English
q:
Read the sentence. Identify the adverb of frequency.
I usually meet Lily for lunch on the weekend.
skill: Always and Never - Identify and use adverbs of frequency
feedback: The correct answer is "usually". The adverb of frequency is "usually".

subject: English
q: What does the adverb of place change?
skill: There's a Time and Place - Identify and use adverbs of time and place
feedback: The correct answer is "the main verb or object". The adverb of place changes the main verb or object only. It does not change adjective or other adverbs.

subject: English
q:
Choose the correct adverb of place.
At nine o'clock, he went to bed _____.
skill: There's a Time and Place - Identify and use adverbs of time and place
feedback: The correct answer is "upstairs". The adverb of place "upstairs" tells us where his bed is.

subject: English
q:
Choose the correct adverb of place.
We have to play _____ on rainy days. We have to stay dry.
skill: There's a Time and Place - Identify and use adverbs of time and place
feedback: The correct answer is "inside". The adverb of place "inside". It is where they have to play to stay dry.

subject: English
q:
Choose the correct adverb of time.
Get up! You've slept ______.
skill: There's a Time and Place - Identify and use adverbs of time and place
feedback: The correct answer is "all day". The adverb of time "all day" tells us how long the person slept for. "Many hours" is incorrect because we need to add "for" before the phrase "many hours" to make it into an adverb of time - "for many hours".

subject: English
q:
Choose the correct adverb of time.
I need your answer _____, not tomorrow.
skill: There's a Time and Place - Identify and use adverbs of time and place
feedback: The correct answer is "today". The adverb of time "today" tells us when the speaker needs an answer. The sentence is in present tense and the answer cannot be "tomorrow".

subject: English
q: Which sentence does not contain an adverb of time?
options: I haven't been back for a year.
I moved out six years ago.
skill: There's a Time and Place - Identify and use adverbs of time and place
feedback: The correct answer is "I haven't been outside." The word "outside" is an adverb of place.

subject: English
q: Which sentence contains an adverb of place?
options: She ran out of milk today.
She went to a nearby shop.
She had to get milk now.
skill: There's a Time and Place - Identify and use adverbs of time and place
feedback: The correct answer is "She spilled the milk everywhere." The adverb of place is "everywhere" and changes the main verb "spill". The word "nearby" is used as an adjective to describe "a shop".

subject: English
q:
Read the sentence. Identify the adverb of place.
I moved the cups around.
skill: There's a Time and Place - Identify and use adverbs of time and place
feedback: The correct answer is "around". The adverb of place is "around".

subject: English
q:
Read the sentence. Identify the adverb of time.
I went to college for four years.
skill: There's a Time and Place - Identify and use adverbs of time and place
feedback: The correct answer is "for four years". The adverb of time is for + an expression of time, "for four years".

subject: English
q:
Read the sentence. Identify the adverb of time.
I have to leave immediately.
skill: There's a Time and Place - Identify and use adverbs of time and place
feedback: The correct answer is "immediately". The adverb of time is "immediately".

subject: English
q: An informal email is _________________.
options: friendly, casual and has stronger emotions
detailed, straight to the point and has stronger emotions
friendly, casual, and has weaker emotions
friendly, straight to the point, and has weaker emotions
skill: A Robo-mail - Write informal emails to provide information, explain a situation and express points of view
feedback: The correct answer is " friendly, casual and has stronger emotions". An informal email is written to people we know like family and friends. We can be friendly, casual and show our real emotions.

subject: English
q: Which sentence would you use in an informal email to show shock?
options: I was surprised to learn about...
The news came as a complete shock.
skill: A Robo-mail - Write informal emails to provide information, explain a situation and express points of view
feedback: The correct answer is <b>"I can't believe it!"<b> The sentence uses a shortened form of a word and an exclamation mark. The other sentences are more formal ways to describe the same emotion.

subject: English
q: Which sentence would you use in a formal email to turn down an invitation?
options: I regret that I will be unable to attend the party.
I'm sorry I can't come for your party.
Wish I could, but it's not possible for me to attend!
skill: A Robo-mail - Write informal emails to provide information, explain a situation and express points of view
feedback: The correct answer is <b>"I regret that I will be unable to attend the party."<b> It is a formal way to turn down an invitation. The other options used shortened forms of words and exclamation marks.

subject: English
q: Which sentence would you use in an informal email?
options: I look forward to hearing from you.
I hope to hear from you at your earliest convenience.
Please do not hesitate to contact us.
skill: A Robo-mail - Write informal emails to provide information, explain a situation and express points of view
feedback: The correct answer is <b>"Looking forward to seeing you!"<b> It is an informal way to close an email. The language is casual and there's a use of the exclamation mark.

subject: English
q: Which sentence would you use in a formal email?
options: I look forward to meeting you.
Hope to hear from you soon!
I can't wait to meet up.
skill: A Robo-mail - Write informal emails to provide information, explain a situation and express points of view
feedback: The correct answer is <b>"I look forward to meeting you."<b> It is a formal way to close an email. The other options are informal ways to close an email.

subject: English
q: Which sentence would you use in an informal email?
options: I am writing to request information about...
I am writing to inform you about...
skill: A Robo-mail - Write informal emails to provide information, explain a situation and express points of view
feedback: The correct answer is " Guess what?" "Guess what?" is an informal way to start an email. The other options are formal ways to start an email.

subject: English
q: Which can be both an informal and formal sign-off?
skill: A Robo-mail - Write informal emails to provide information, explain a situation and express points of view
feedback: The correct answer is " Cheers". "Cheers" can be used in an informal or formal email.

subject: English
q: Which is an informal sign-off?
skill: A Robo-mail - Write informal emails to provide information, explain a situation and express points of view
feedback: The correct answer is " Love". "Love" is an informal way to sign off.

subject: English
q: Which is a formal greeting?
skill: A Robo-mail - Write informal emails to provide information, explain a situation and express points of view
feedback: The correct answer is " Dear Mr. Brown". "Dear" can be used in both formal and informal emails, but it is very formal when we address a person by their last name, as in "Mr. Brown".

subject: English
q: What does direct speech tell us?
topic: Reinventing the Wheel Part 1 - Recall the rules of punctuation learnt earlier; identify and write direct speech to state something or ask a question
skill: There is no need to shout. _____ can hear you.
feedback: The correct answer is " the speaker's exact words". Direct speech tells us the exact words of the speaker.

subject: English
q:
Where do we place a question mark in this sentence?
"Shall we go" asked Mom.
topic: Reinventing the Wheel Part 1 - Recall the rules of punctuation learnt earlier; identify and write direct speech to state something or ask a question
skill: Two rational numbers are reciprocals of each other if their
feedback: The correct answer is "Shall we go?" asked Mom. The question mark should be added to the end of the speech line, inside the speech mark. A speech must end with a punctuation.

subject: English
q:
Where do we place a comma in this sentence?
"I'd like a coffee" said Jim.
topic: Reinventing the Wheel Part 1 - Recall the rules of punctuation learnt earlier; identify and write direct speech to state something or ask a question
skill: Which of the following are the factors of \[(x^{6}-4)\]?
feedback: The correct answer is "I'd like a coffee," said Jim. The comma should be added to the end of the speech line, inside the speech mark. A speech must end with a punctuation.

subject: English
q:
Where do we place the speech marks in this sentence?
I wish you were here, said Kim.
options: "I wish you were here," said Kim.
"I wish you were here, said Kim".
"I wish" you were here, said Kim.
I wish you were here, "said Kim".
topic: Reinventing the Wheel Part 1 - Recall the rules of punctuation learnt earlier; identify and write direct speech to state something or ask a question
feedback: The correct answer is "I wish you were here," said Kim. The speech marks are placed around the speech line.

subject: English
q:
Read the sentence and identify the reporting clause.
Dad said, "Fine. You may play outside for 20 minutes."
topic: Reinventing the Wheel Part 1 - Recall the rules of punctuation learnt earlier; identify and write direct speech to state something or ask a question
feedback: The correct answer is "shouted Greg" The reporting clause is "Dad said".

subject: English
q:
Read the sentence and identify the reporting clause.
"Quick! Pass me the ball," shouted Greg.
topic: Reinventing the Wheel Part 1 - Recall the rules of punctuation learnt earlier; identify and write direct speech to state something or ask a question
feedback: The correct answer is "shouted Greg" The reporting clause is "shouted Greg".

subject: English
q: In a story, when does a character use indirect speech?
options: when having a conversation with someone in person
when having a conversation with someone on the phone
when talking about what someone might say in the future
when talking about what someone said in the past
topic: Reinventing the Wheel Part 2 - Identify and write direct and indirect speech to state/negate something, or ask a question
feedback: The correct answer is "when talking about what someone said in the past". Indirect speech is often used when talking about a conversation that happened in the past.

subject: English
q:
What is the following sentence in indirect speech?
"Where are we going?" Millie asked.
options: Millie asked where are we going?
Millie asked if we were going.
Millie asked where we were going.
Millie asked where were we going?
topic: Reinventing the Wheel Part 2 - Identify and write direct and indirect speech to state/negate something, or ask a question
feedback: The correct answer is "Millie asked where we were going." The sentence is a wh- question, so the reporting clause is followed by a clause that starts with wh-. It is not a question so there's no need for a question mark and the helping verb "were" comes after the subject "we".

subject: English
q:
What is the following sentence in indirect speech?
"Grandma called!" Sally said.
topic: Reinventing the Wheel Part 2 - Identify and write direct and indirect speech to state/negate something, or ask a question
feedback: The correct answer is "Sally said that Grandma called." The sentence is a statement, so the reporting clause is followed by the that-clause.

subject: English
q:
What is the following sentence in indirect speech?
"Stand here," the teacher said.
options: The teacher told him to stand here.
The teacher said to him, "Stand here".
topic: Reinventing the Wheel Part 2 - Identify and write direct and indirect speech to state/negate something, or ask a question
feedback: The correct answer is "The teacher told him to stand to stand here." Indirect speech does not use speech marks.

subject: English
q:
Read the sentence and identify the reporting clause.
He told us to run and hide.
topic: Reinventing the Wheel Part 2 - Identify and write direct and indirect speech to state/negate something, or ask a question
feedback: The correct answer is “He told us”. The reporting clause is “He told us”. The reported clause that follows is “to run and hide”.

subject: English
q:
Read the sentence and identify the reporting clause.
The doctor said that you should eat more fruits and vegetables.
topic: Reinventing the Wheel Part 2 - Identify and write direct and indirect speech to state/negate something, or ask a question
feedback: The correct answer is "The doctor said". The reporting clause is "The doctor said". The clause that follows is "that you should eat more fruits and vegetables".

subject: English
q:
Read the sentences below. What does the word "impact" mean?

In 1990, Cousteau got most developed countries to agree not to drill for oil in Antarctica. It would have a serious impact on the ice shelves and the wildlife.
skill: An Environmental Hero - Identify the purpose and audience of biographies; guess meaning from the context
feedback: The correct answer is, "effect". Cousteau knew that drilling in Antarctica would disturb the land and the wildlife. It would have a very bad result or effect on the environment.

subject: English
q:
Choose the right answer to complete the sentence below.

In 1960, French atomic _____ wanted to dump _____ from the _____ power plant in Marcoule into the Mediterranean Sea.
skill: An Environmental Hero - Identify the purpose and audience of biographies; guess meaning from the context
feedback: The correct answer is "scientists, waste, nuclear". The atomic scientists did not think that waste from the nuclear power plant would harm the Mediterranean Sea, but Jacques Cousteau could prove they were wrong, and the public believed him.

subject: English
q: What effect does the story about the people who stopped the train by lying on the tracks have on readers of this biography?
options: It gives straight facts with no emotion.
Readers connect emotionally with the event.
It explains how hard Cousteau worked to protect the sea.
It gives readers an idea of the importance of energy.
skill: An Environmental Hero - Identify the purpose and audience of biographies; guess meaning from the context
feedback: The correct answer is "Readers connect emotionally with the event". Readers can picture how people risked their lives to lie down on the railroad tracks as the train came toward them. They really believed that protecting the sea was extremely important, so they were very brave to do that.

subject: English
q: In what way was it "lucky" that Jacques Cousteau was in a car accident that almost killed him?
options: He decided that working for the French Navy as a photographer was more interesting than becoming a pilot.
He decided to drive ships and submarines instead of cars because they were safer.
He would never have invented SCUBA or taken up the cause of protecting the oceans.
It taught him that he had to be very careful when he drove from that time forward.
skill: An Environmental Hero - Identify the purpose and audience of biographies; guess meaning from the context
feedback: The correct answer is "He would never have invented SCUBA or taken up the cause of protecting the oceans." His injuries kept him from becoming a pilot, but they did not affect his ability to dive or invent things.

subject: English
q: Which of the following was NOT an invention of Jacques Cousteau?
skill: An Environmental Hero - Identify the purpose and audience of biographies; guess meaning from the context
feedback: The correct answer is "underwater goggles". They already existed when Cousteau was a young man.

subject: English
q: What were young Jacques Cousteau's interests when he was young?
options: diving, inventing things and watching movies
swimming, taking pictures and making movies
studying hard and making model airplanes
skill: An Environmental Hero - Identify the purpose and audience of biographies; guess meaning from the context
feedback: The correct answer is "swimming, taking pictures and making movies". These interests prepared him to invent diving equipment so that he could swim underwater, take pictures and make movies of what he saw.

subject: English
q: What kind of student was Jacques Cousteau as a child?
options: He did well but was not outstanding.
He was at the top of his class.
He was not a very good student.
skill: An Environmental Hero - Identify the purpose and audience of biographies; guess meaning from the context
feedback: The correct answer is "He was not a very good student". In fact, he was very sickly as a child and was not interested in going to school.

subject: English
q: What is NOT a reason why the mountain guides are the heroes in this tragic earthquake?
options: The guides risked their lives to help others.
The guides worked continuously to save lives.
The guides were the first rescuers.
The guides were very good at climbing Mount Kibu.
skill: A Close Shave - Identify the features of a newspaper article; summarise its main ideas and key details and form opinions about it
feedback: The correct answer is "The guides were very good at climbing Mount Kibu." This is a skill the mountain guides have, not a quality that makes them heroes.

subject: English
q:
Complete the sentence based on the news article.

Gilly was "a survivor who escaped unharmed". A "survivor" is a person who__________ after an event in which others have died.
skill: A Close Shave - Identify the features of a newspaper article; summarise its main ideas and key details and form opinions about it
feedback: The correct answer is "remains alive". A "survivor" is a person who remains alive after an event in which others have died.

subject: English
q: What was Gilly grateful about?
options: She survived the earthquake without a scratch.
She had a broken arm but was saved by the guides.
She sprained her ankle, and her guide carried her down the mountain.
She was trapped under a tree and rescued by the search party.
skill: A Close Shave - Identify the features of a newspaper article; summarise its main ideas and key details and form opinions about it
feedback: The correct answer is "She survived the earthquake without a scratch". Gilly escaped the earthquake unharmed and is "forever grateful".

subject: English
q:
What does this sentence mean?

Unfortunately, we could not save everyone.
options: Some people escaped on their own.
skill: A Close Shave - Identify the features of a newspaper article; summarise its main ideas and key details and form opinions about it
feedback: The correct answer is "Some people died". The guides tried to save as many lives as possible, but could not save everyone. The article says that 13 people have died.

subject: English
q: Why were people trapped at the top of the mountain?
options: They were too injured to climb.
They had no energy left to climb.
They were lost and were not with their guides.
They had no way to climb down.
skill: A Close Shave - Identify the features of a newspaper article; summarise its main ideas and key details and form opinions about it
feedback: The correct answer is "They had no way to climb down". The landslide had blocked many paths. There was no way down the mountain for those trapped at the top.

subject: English
q: How was Timor injured?
options: A plate hit him in the head.
A shield hit him in the head.
A rock hit him in the head.
A rock hit him in the back.
skill: A Close Shave - Identify the features of a newspaper article; summarise its main ideas and key details and form opinions about it
feedback: The correct answer is "A rock hit him in the head". A rock the size of a dinner plate hit Timor in the head. The picture shows that he was bleeding.

subject: English
q: When was the last time the city of Sandan experienced such an earthquake?
skill: A Close Shave - Identify the features of a newspaper article; summarise its main ideas and key details and form opinions about it
feedback: The correct answer is "30 years ago". They had not experienced an earthquake that strong in the past 30 years.

subject: English
q: What struck the city of Sandan?
skill: A Close Shave - Identify the features of a newspaper article; summarise its main ideas and key details and form opinions about it
feedback: The correct answer is "a 5.9-magnitude earthquake". A 5.9-magnitude earthquake struck the city of Sandan, near Mount Kibu.

subject: English
q: The indirect object comes after the direct object _____
options: every time we use an indirect object.
when you use it with a prepositional phrase with "to" only.
when you use it with a prepositional phrase with "for" only.
only if we use it with a prepositional phrase with "to" or "for".
skill: The Game-winning Goal - Recognize and use simple SVO pattern sentences with direct and indirect objects
feedback: The correct answer is "only if we use it with a prepositional phrase with "to" or "for." We use a prepositional phrase "to/for + indirect object" after the direct object in an SVO sentence.

subject: English
q:
Complete this sentence with an indirect object.
I brought a pie __________.
skill: The Game-winning Goal - Recognize and use simple SVO pattern sentences with direct and indirect objects
feedback: The correct answer is "to her house." The direct object comes before the indirect object so we use a prepositional phrase in this sentence. We use "to + indirect object" with the verb "brought".

subject: English
q:
Complete this sentence with an indirect object.
I saved a piece of cake __________.
skill: The Game-winning Goal - Recognize and use simple SVO pattern sentences with direct and indirect objects
feedback: The correct answer is "for my little sister." The direct object comes before the indirect object so we use a prepositional phrase in this sentence. We use "for + indirect object" with the verb "saved".

subject: English
q:
Complete this sentence.
I made _____ for _____.
skill: The Game-winning Goal - Recognize and use simple SVO pattern sentences with direct and indirect objects
feedback: The correct answer is "dinner, us" The sentence is missing a direct object and an indirect object.

subject: English
q:
Complete this sentence.
I wish you _____.
skill: The Game-winning Goal - Recognize and use simple SVO pattern sentences with direct and indirect objects
feedback: The correct answer is "luck". The sentence is missing an object.

subject: English
q:
Read the sentence below and identify the direct object.
The children picked flowers for their mother.
skill: The Game-winning Goal - Recognize and use simple SVO pattern sentences with direct and indirect objects
feedback: The correct answer is "flowers". The direct object is "flowers".

subject: English
q:
Read the sentence below and identify the indirect object.
The dog brought a stick to its owner.
skill: The Game-winning Goal - Recognize and use simple SVO pattern sentences with direct and indirect objects
feedback: The correct answer is "owner". The indirect object is "owner".

subject: English
q:
Read the sentence below and identify the direct object and the indirect object.
Our teacher is buying us lunch.
skill: The Game-winning Goal - Recognize and use simple SVO pattern sentences with direct and indirect objects
feedback: The correct answer is "lunch, us". The direct object is "lunch". The indirect object is "us".

subject: English
q:
Read the sentence below and identify the direct object.
He gave Mom a gift.
skill: The Game-winning Goal - Recognize and use simple SVO pattern sentences with direct and indirect objects
feedback: The correct answer is "a gift". The direct object is "a gift".

subject: English
q:
Read the sentence below and identify the indirect object.
She lent me a movie.
skill: The Game-winning Goal - Recognize and use simple SVO pattern sentences with direct and indirect objects
feedback: The correct answer is "me". The indirect object is "me".

subject: English
q: When do we use both "shall" and "will"?
options: When talking about a future that is certain.
When talking about a present that is certain.
When making a request or an order
topic: modal verbs for prediction, intention and determination
skill: It's a Deal - Use modal verbs shall and will for prediction, intention and determination
feedback: The correct answer is "When talking about a future that is certain.". Both "shall" and "will" can be used in a way that can be exchanged.

subject: English
q:
Choose the correct modal verb and complete the following suggestion.

_____ I send you the book? I want you to read it.
topic: modal verbs for prediction, intention and determination
skill: It's a Deal - Use modal verbs shall and will for prediction, intention and determination
feedback: The correct answer is "shall". "Shall I send you the book?" is a suggestion.

subject: English
q:
Choose the correct modal verb and complete the following request.

_____ you close the door please? It's getting cold in here.
topic: modal verbs for prediction, intention and determination
skill: It's a Deal - Use modal verbs shall and will for prediction, intention and determination
feedback: The correct answer is "will". "Will you close the door please?" is a request.

subject: English
q:
Choose the correct modal verb and complete the following promise.

If you play well, _____ get on the team.
topic: modal verbs for prediction, intention and determination
skill: It's a Deal - Use modal verbs shall and will for prediction, intention and determination
feedback: The correct answer is "you'll". In this sentence, "will" is used as a promise. If you play well, I promise that you'll get on the team.

subject: English
q:
Choose the correct modal verb and complete the following refusal.

I _____ go for a hike with them tomorrow. I am going fishing instead.
topic: modal verbs for prediction, intention and determination
skill: It's a Deal - Use modal verbs shall and will for prediction, intention and determination
feedback: The correct answer is "won't". In this sentence, "will" is used to make a refusal. I will refuse to hike with them tomorrow.

subject: English
q:
Choose the correct modal verb and complete the following prediction.

I _____ be ten years old in two months.
topic: modal verbs for prediction, intention and determination
skill: It's a Deal - Use modal verbs shall and will for prediction, intention and determination
feedback: The correct answer is "will". "I will be ten years old in two month." "Will" talks about a future that is certain.

subject: English
q: Which sentence uses the modal verb to make a request?
options: I shall give him an apple.
Shall we bake a cake for Mom?
Will you teach me how to cook?
topic: modal verbs for prediction, intention and determination
skill: It's a Deal - Use modal verbs shall and will for prediction, intention and determination
feedback: The correct answer is "Will you teach me how to cook?" "Will" in a question makes a request.

subject: English
q: Which sentence uses the modal verb to make a suggestion?
options: I shall take a nap when I get home.
Shall we go to the park tomorrow?
Will we visit Grandma on the weekend?
We will go to the beach next week.
topic: modal verbs for prediction, intention and determination
skill: It's a Deal - Use modal verbs shall and will for prediction, intention and determination
feedback: The correct answer is "Shall we go to the park tomorrow?" "Shall" in a question makes a suggestion.

subject: English
q: Which sentence uses the modal verb to make a refusal?
topic: modal verbs for prediction, intention and determination
skill: It's a Deal - Use modal verbs shall and will for prediction, intention and determination
feedback: The correct answer is "I won't give you candy." "Won't" the shortened form of "will not" is used to refuse or reject.

subject: English
q: Which sentence uses the modal verb to make an offer?
options: He won't give you his toy.
Will we go to the store?
topic: modal verbs for prediction, intention and determination
skill: It's a Deal - Use modal verbs shall and will for prediction, intention and determination
feedback: The correct answer is "I shall help you clean." "Will" and "shall" are both used to make an offer.

subject: English
q: Which of the following is a suffix in the word preparing?
skill: The Lantern Festival - Recognize common affixes; read a magazine article
feedback: The correct answer is "-ing". The word "preparing" has a root word prepare. It has a suffix -ing, which means an act of doing something.

subject: English
q: Why do some people put the dolls into the river?
options: to wish that their girls will be pretty
to wish that bad luck will go away
skill: The Lantern Festival - Recognize common affixes; read a magazine article
feedback: The correct answer is "to wish that bad luck will go away". People put the dolls into the river to pray for their girl's health and good luck. "Mis-" is a common prefix that means wrong, and "dis-" means opposite.

subject: English
q: Why do families store the dolls away after the festival?
options: They want to use them again next year.
They want to sell them for a good price.
The dolls are no longer useful.
The dolls are to be thrown away later.
skill: The Lantern Festival - Recognize common affixes; read a magazine article
feedback: The correct answer is "They want to use them again next year". The word "reuse" means to use something again. "Re-" is a common prefix that means again.

subject: English
q: What does the word "decorate" mean in the second paragraph?
options: to take part in an event
skill: The Lantern Festival - Recognize common affixes; read a magazine article
feedback: The correct answer is "to make something more beautiful". The doll display is a holiday decoration. It makes the house more beautiful and joyous.

subject: English
q: What does "fortune" mean in the first paragraph?
skill: The Lantern Festival - Recognize common affixes; read a magazine article
feedback: The correct answer is "luck". On Girl's Day, people wish for their girl's happiness and good luck.

subject: English
q: What kinds of activities take place on the Girl's Day?
options: People release lanterns into the river.
People put rice crackers into the river. .
skill: The Lantern Festival - Recognize common affixes; read a magazine article
feedback: The correct answer is "Families put out doll displays". On this day, people decorate their homes with five-to-seven-tiered doll displays.

subject: English
q: Why is the Girl's Day celebrated in Japan?
options: People celebrate this day to wish for the happiness of the girls in the family.
Girls celebrate this day to honour a famous princess.
People celebrate this day to welcome new-born baby girls into their family.
Girls celebrate this day to have parties with their friends.
skill: The Lantern Festival - Recognize common affixes; read a magazine article
feedback: The correct answer is "People celebrate this day to wish for the happiness of the girls in the family." On this day, families pray that the diseases and misfortune will disappear from their daughter's life.

subject: English
q: Which of the following words or phrases has the same meaning as may/might?
skill: Art Appreciation - Use modal verbs may, can, could, might, must to convey possibility
feedback: The correct answer is "could be". "Could be" can be used to replace "may" or "might".

subject: English
q: Which of the following sentences expresses general possibility?
options: This must be her book; her name is written on the cover.
Climbing mountains in winter can be very difficult.
skill: Art Appreciation - Use modal verbs may, can, could, might, must to convey possibility
feedback: The correct answer is "Climbing mountains in winter can be very difficult". The modal verb can is used to express general possibility about something that takes place all the time.

subject: English
q:
Which sentence has the same meaning as the sentence below?

I am not sure if they went hiking last week.
options: They might have gone hiking last week.
They must have gone hiking last week.
They may have gone hiking last week.
They should have gone hiking last week.
skill: Art Appreciation - Use modal verbs may, can, could, might, must to convey possibility
feedback: The correct answer is "They might have gone hiking last week." We use modal verbs "might", "may", and "must" + have + past participles to make guesses about the past.

subject: English
q:
Which sentence has the same meaning as the sentence below?

It is highly possible that Sarah needs your help in the kitchen.
options: Sarah must need your help in the kitchen.
Sarah may need your help in the kitchen.
Sarah might need your help in the kitchen.
Sarah could use your help in the kitchen.
skill: Art Appreciation - Use modal verbs may, can, could, might, must to convey possibility
feedback: The correct answer is "Sarah may need your help in the kitchen." The modal verb may is used when something is likely to happen.

subject: English
q:
Which sentence has the same meaning as the sentence below?

There is a slight chance that it will rain tomorrow.
skill: Art Appreciation - Use modal verbs may, can, could, might, must to convey possibility
feedback: The correct answer is "It might rain tomorrow." The modal verb might is used when there is a slight possibility for something to take place.

subject: English
q:
Which sentence has the same meaning as the sentence below?

I am certain that these numbers are accurate.
skill: Art Appreciation - Use modal verbs may, can, could, might, must to convey possibility
feedback: The correct answer is "These numbers must be accurate." The modal verb must is used when we are very certain that our claim is true.

subject: English
q: Which sentence is NOT a guess?
options: She might invite Sofia to her birthday party.
I might go for a walk later.
She can play football really well.
We could be late for the meeting if we don't hurry up.
skill: Art Appreciation - Use modal verbs may, can, could, might, must to convey possibility
feedback: The correct answer is "She can play football really well." The modal verb can In this sentence shows "ability" not "possibility".

subject: English
q: Which sentence makes a guess about a future event?
options: He must have gone to school by now.
Ali might be playing cricket at the moment.
She could be the one performing at the music festival.
They might have gone to the wrong house.
skill: Art Appreciation - Use modal verbs may, can, could, might, must to convey possibility
feedback: The correct answer is "She could be the one performing at the music festival." The sentence means that it is quite likely that she will perform at the upcoming music festival.

subject: English
q: Which sentence makes a guess about a past event?
options: He may drive Ben to school today.
He might have taken the test already.
He could be sleeping right now.
She might go to Jane's house in the evening.
skill: Art Appreciation - Use modal verbs may, can, could, might, must to convey possibility
feedback: The correct answer is "He might have taken the test already." To make a guess about a past event, we use modal verbs "might", "may", and "must" with have + past participle.

subject: English
q: Which sentence uses the modal verb to make a guess?
options: He was driving to work yesterday.
He will be driving to work tomorrow.
He must be driving to work right now.
He is driving to work right now.
skill: Art Appreciation - Use modal verbs may, can, could, might, must to convey possibility
feedback: The correct answer is "He must be driving to work right now." The modal verbs may, might, can, could, and must are used to talk about possibilities.

subject: English
q:
Read the sentence below and replace the underlined word with a simile.

Her eyes are bright.
skill: My Favourite Festival - Analyse and use similes to describe a festival
feedback: The correct answer is "like diamonds". This simile compares her eyes to diamonds to illustrate how bright her eyes are.

subject: English
q:
Read the sentence below and replace the underlined phrases with a simile.

It is extremely hot in here.
options: like a cup of hot cocoa on a snowy day
as comfortable as a freshly made bed
skill: My Favourite Festival - Analyse and use similes to describe a festival
feedback: The correct answer is "like a furnace". It is as hot as a furnace in here. A furnace is a structure in which heat is produced by burning fuel. When a place is compared to a furnace, it means that the place is extremely hot.

subject: English
q: Choose the correct definition of the simile "to slither like a snake".
options: to move in an S shape
skill: My Favourite Festival - Analyse and use similes to describe a festival
feedback: The correct answer is "to move in an S shape". For example, I lay on my stomach and slithered like a snake to get under the bed.

subject: English
q:
Complete the sentence below.

I bought a second-hand car. It is _____.
options: as noisy as a fighter jet
skill: My Favourite Festival - Analyse and use similes to describe a festival
feedback: The correct answer is "as noisy as a fighter jet". I bought a second-hand car. It is as noisy as a fighter jet. The sentence compares the noise made by the car to that of a fighter jet.

subject: English
q:
Complete the sentence below.

He is so quiet; he moves like _____.
skill: My Favourite Festival - Analyse and use similes to describe a festival
feedback: The correct answer is "a ghost in the wind". He is so quiet; he moves like a ghost in the wind. This simile compares his movement to that of a ghost gliding in the wind. It emphasizes how quietly he moves.

subject: English
q:
Complete the sentence below.

After the final exam, I felt as free as a _____.
skill: My Favourite Festival - Analyse and use similes to describe a festival
feedback: The correct answer is "bird". After the final exam, I felt as free as a bird. This simile means feeling completely free with no worries.

subject: English
q: Which of the following is not a simile?
options: I slept like a baby last night.
The math test is as easy as A.B.C.
There are stars in her eyes.
Amber ran as fast as the wind.
skill: My Favourite Festival - Analyse and use similes to describe a festival
feedback: The correct answer is "There are stars in her eyes." This sentence is not a simile as it does not use "like" or "as...as" to compare two different things. We call sentences like this a metaphor. It implies that the light in her eyes are shining like stars.

subject: English
q: Which of the following is NOT a purpose of using similes in writing or speech?
options: to add depth to the description
to paint a vivid picture in the reader's mind
to explain a concept quickly and effectively
skill: My Favourite Festival - Analyse and use similes to describe a festival
feedback: The correct answer is "to create a formal tone". Similes add depth to the description and paint a vivid picture. It is also a convenient tool to explain a new concept. In addition, similes are often used in descriptive or narrative texts.

subject: English
q: What type of words do we often use between the joining words "as ... as" to form similes?
skill: My Favourite Festival - Analyse and use similes to describe a festival
feedback: The correct answer is "adjectives". We often use adjectives to show the shared quality of the two things being compared. For example, the cake smells as sweet as April. The word "sweet" is an adjective. It is a quality shared by the smell of the cake and April.

subject: English
q:
Complete the definition below.

A simile _____
options: uses "like" and "as...as" to compare humans to animals.
compares two similar things with "like" and "as...as".
compares two unlike things with "like" and "as...as".
skill: My Favourite Festival - Analyse and use similes to describe a festival
feedback: The correct answer is "compares two unlike things with 'like' and 'as...as'". A simile compares two things that are not similar with "like" and "as...as". It is a type of figurative language.
